title: Case statement incorrectly matches branch when using signed 1-bit input
body:
Below is the code that triggers the problem (`test.v`):

```verilog
module test;

reg signed in4;
initial begin
  in4 = 1;

  #1;

  $display("in4: %0d", in4);

  case (0 ? 1'h0 : in4)
    5'b0101: begin
        $display("1");
    end

    8'b000001: begin
      $display("2");
    end

    default: begin
      $display("3");
    end
  endcase
end

endmodule
```

Run the commands below to reproduce:

```bash
verilator --binary -Wno-lint --timing -fno-expand test.v
./obj_dir/Vtest
```

The program prints

```
in4: -1
2
```

According to my understanding, the ternary operator should choose `in4`, because `0` is `false`.  
`in4` is an **signed** value and its binary representation is `1'b1`.  
When it is compared with `8'b000001` in the `case`, it will be sign-extended to `8'b11111111`, so it should not match that branch (nor the other branch). Therefore the default branch ought to execute, printing `3` instead of `2` (This behavior  (**printing 3**) is also observed when using Icarus Verilog.).

I also tried disabling optimizations with `-fno-expand`, but the output is still `2`.  
Because the result does not depend on the use of `$display`, this seems unrelated to <https://github.com/verilator/verilator/issues/5963>, so I’m opening a new issue here.
